title: This Was a Big Week for Marie Curie, More Than 120 Years Ago
gist: A historical retrospective on Marie Curie, who on June 25, 1903 became the first woman in France to earn a science doctorate — months before her first Nobel — overcoming national disadvantage, lost family fortunes, and barred access to higher education in Poland.

insights.claim: Marie Curie remains the only person to win two science Nobel Prizes in different fields — Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911 — and was the first woman to win a Nobel Prize.
insights.speaker: Bob Grant
insights.so_what: Historical reference point on singular scientific achievement; no market read.
insights.quote: she is still the only person to win two science Nobels in different fields
insights.locator: She was not only the first woman to win a Nobel Prize

insights.claim: Curie became the first woman in France awarded a doctorate in the sciences on June 25, 1903, just six months before her first Nobel Prize, which she won for the same defended thesis research.
insights.speaker: Bob Grant
insights.so_what: Underscores how compressed her landmark achievements were.
insights.quote: That accomplishment came on June 25, 1903, a mere six months before she would win her first Nobel Prize

insights.claim: Curie overcame structural barriers — lost family fortunes from anti-Imperial-Russia movements in Poland and a ban on women attending Polish universities — to achieve her firsts.
insights.speaker: Bob Grant
insights.so_what: Context on systemic obstacles to her career.
insights.quote: She wasn’t even allowed to attend university in Poland as women were prohibited from pursuing higher education.
insights.locator: After her being born Maria Skłodowska in 1867
